_meta.purpose: STAGING candidates for P8 (KB content mining, slice: thicken 1945-1972). NOT live data. Owner reviews before any merge into data/events/historical_events.json.
_meta.schema_note: Each object matches the live historical_events.json event shape (id,title,description,category,era{earliest,latest},risk{riskLevel,returnRange,successProbability,duration,failureLoss,windfallProbability,windfallMultiplier},affects{benefits,suffers},weight,historicalNote) PLUS a non-schema 'provenance' block and 'draft_quality' (1-5). Strip 'provenance' and 'draft_quality' before merging.
_meta.division_constraints_applied: Benefits/suffers restricted to divisions plausibly available 1945-1972: CommercialLending, MortgageLending, TrustAndCustody, CreditCards(1958+), InternationalBanking. TradingDesk and InvestmentBanking deliberately avoided pre-~1973 per Division.h era gates (existing live events do reference them pre-1973, but affects on an un-unlocked division are inert; candidates here stay era-clean). See REVIEW_SHEET.md.
_meta.id_prefix: evt_kb_ marks these as mined candidates; rename on merge if desired.
_meta.existing_coverage_avoided: Does not duplicate the 17 live 1945-1972 events (ve_day_conversion, bretton_woods_order, gi_bill_mortgages, regulation_q, cold_war_deposits, korean_war_bonds, suburban_boom, treasury_accord, eisenhower_highway, eurodollar_birth, credit_card_revolution, kennedy_tax_cut, vietnam_inflation, conglomerate_wave, credit_crunch_1966, penn_central, gold_window).

candidates.title: The Reconversion Credit Squeeze
candidates.description: War contracts have evaporated overnight and factories sit half-idle between the last shell order and the first new Buick. Manufacturers are desperate for working capital to retool, but nobody knows which firms will survive the switch to civilian goods. Lend boldly into the chaos and you anchor a client for thirty years — or you fund a plant that never reopens.
candidates.historicalNote: Industrial production fell sharply in 1945-46 as war orders ceased before civilian demand absorbed the slack; banks that financed retooling captured durable corporate relationships.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: general postwar reconversion history; Levy KB extraction did not reach postwar chapters

candidates.title: The Great War-Bond Unwind
candidates.description: Households are sitting on a mountain of Series E war bonds and they are starting to cash them in for refrigerators and down payments. Deposits are sloshing in and out unpredictably. A trust desk that helps wealthy families reposition maturing Treasuries into managed portfolios earns steady, unglamorous fees — the kind your board adores.
candidates.historicalNote: Americans bought enormous volumes of war bonds; their postwar redemption fed both consumer spending and a wave of portfolio repositioning that benefited trust departments.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: general; war-bond redemption not detailed in KB extractions

candidates.title: Even Keel: Financing the Treasury
candidates.description: The Federal Reserve still smooths every Treasury refunding, holding rates steady whenever Washington brings a new issue to market. Your government-bond portfolio is safe as a vault — for now. But the pegging cannot last forever, and the bank that loads up on long bonds at today's frozen yields may regret it when the dam breaks.
candidates.historicalNote: Before the 1951 Accord the Fed pegged Treasury bond prices; bondholders could sell to the Fed at fixed rates without loss — an implicit subsidy that vanished once pegging ended.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: chunk 007-008, p.49 (even-keel policy; holders could no longer sell bonds to the Fed without loss)

candidates.title: Regulation W Tightens the Installment Plan
candidates.description: With Korea heating up, the Fed has revived Regulation W — minimum down payments and maximum repayment terms on every washing machine and Studebaker bought on time. Your consumer-finance pipeline just got throttled by Washington. Stay clean and lose volume, or get creative about what counts as a 'down payment' and risk the examiners.
candidates.historicalNote: Selective credit controls (Regulation W on consumer installment credit, Regulation X on real estate) were imposed during Korean War mobilization and ended in January 1952, after which bank loan demand rose.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: chunk 007, p.55 (end of selective credit controls Jan 1952, line 120); footnote 18 p.60 (line 217). Detail thin in KB — Reg W/X mechanics supplemented from general knowledge

candidates.title: Regulation X Reins In the Builders
candidates.description: Real-estate credit controls have arrived: bigger down payments, shorter mortgage terms, on the theory that a wartime economy cannot pour concrete and cannons at once. Your mortgage desk's volume sags just as the suburbs are ready to explode. The builders who can still find financing will owe you for years.
candidates.historicalNote: Regulation X imposed real-estate credit restraints during Korean War mobilization, lifted with other selective controls by early 1952.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: chunk 007 (selective credit controls ended Jan 1952); Reg X specifics supplemented from general knowledge

candidates.title: The Pegging Ends — Bonds Reprice
candidates.description: The Federal Reserve has stopped buying Treasury bonds at fixed prices. Overnight, the safest assets on your balance sheet behave like risky ones: long bonds you bought at frozen yields are worth less, and every refunding now clears at a market rate. A nimble bank shortens its book; a sleepy one eats the loss.
candidates.historicalNote: After March 1951 the Fed ceased pegging bond prices; long yields drifted up over the 1950s (a 5% Treasury note — the 'magic 5s' — was issued in 1959), imposing capital losses on banks holding long government paper.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: chunk 007-008, p.49 (holders could no longer sell to Fed without loss); 'magic 5s' 1959

candidates.title: The 1953 Pause
candidates.description: The Korean armistice and a turn to budget restraint have cooled the economy. Loan demand softens and a few cyclical borrowers wobble. It is a mild downturn — nothing like the thirties — but a careful banker trims his riskiest credits now and keeps powder dry for the rebound.
candidates.historicalNote: A mild recession followed the 1953 Korean armistice and a swing toward fiscal restraint; the postwar cycle was shallow by historical standards.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: chunk 007, pp.51-54 (1950s cyclical narrative; surpluses 1956-57)

candidates.title: Bills Only at the Desk
candidates.description: Chairman Martin has the Open Market Desk trading only short Treasury bills, refusing to manage the long end of the curve directly. The yield curve now moves on its own logic, and banks that read it well can position ahead of the Fed. Misjudge where long rates are headed and your bond book pays the price.
candidates.historicalNote: Under Martin the Fed adopted a 'bills only' operating doctrine in the mid-1950s, intervening at the short end and leaving long rates to the market; it was relaxed via interventions in late 1955 and mid-1958.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: chunk 007, pp.50-62 (bills-only; Sproul rivalry; relaxations Nov 1955, July 1958)

candidates.title: The Bank Holding Company Act
candidates.description: Congress has passed the Bank Holding Company Act of 1956 to rein in multi-bank empires — but it pointedly exempts the one-bank holding company. A clever banker forms a single holding company over his bank and slips into businesses the law never meant him to touch. The loophole is wide open; the examiners are watching how brazen you'll be.
candidates.historicalNote: The 1956 Bank Holding Company Act did not cover one-bank holding companies; banks exploited that loophole until the 1970 amendments closed it. The Fed repeatedly sought authority over one-bank holding companies.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: chunk: p.463 (line 940), p.466 (line 996) one-bank holding company loophole; Board sought extension of authority

candidates.title: The Sharp Recession of 1958
candidates.description: The economy has dropped hard — unemployment past seven percent, factories idle, the deepest slump since the war. Politicians are already blaming tight money and 'three recessions in eight years.' Your problem loans are mounting. Provision honestly and survive the audit, or paper over the losses and pray for a fast recovery.
candidates.historicalNote: The 1957-58 recession was the sharpest postwar downturn to that point, with unemployment above 7% in 1958; it became a political cudgel against Eisenhower-era monetary policy.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: chunk 007, pp.51-54 (1957-58 recession); 1960 Democratic platform 'three recessions in eight years' (chunk 029 p.268)

candidates.title: The Negotiable CD Arrives
candidates.description: A New York money-center bank has invented something quietly revolutionary: a large certificate of deposit a corporation can buy, hold, and then sell to someone else before maturity. Suddenly idle corporate cash that fled to Treasury bills can come back onto your balance sheet — if you're bold enough to bid for it. The age of managed liabilities begins at your desk or your rival's.
candidates.historicalNote: The negotiable certificate of deposit (introduced by a major New York bank around 1961) let banks actively bid for corporate funds rather than wait for deposits — the birth of liability management.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: Meltzer KB covers CD/Reg Q circumvention generically (First National Bank of Boston promissory notes 1964) but does NOT contain the specific 1961 First National City negotiable-CD origin; attribution from general knowledge

candidates.title: Regulation Q Bites: Disintermediation
candidates.description: Market interest rates have climbed above the ceiling Regulation Q lets you pay on deposits, and your savers are noticing. Money is walking out the door into Treasury bills and into the thrift down the street that can pay a quarter-point more. Raise rates to the legal limit and squeeze your margin, or watch your funding base bleed away.
candidates.historicalNote: Regulation Q ceilings repeatedly sat below market rates, draining time deposits from banks to thrifts and market instruments — 'disintermediation.' The 1956 ceiling was raised to 3%, and to 5.5% in December 1965.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: chunk 007, p.48 (line 16); pp.454-455 (1956 ceiling 3%, Dec 1965 ceiling 5.5%)

candidates.title: Martin Takes Away the Punch Bowl
candidates.description: Chairman Martin warns that the Fed's job is to remove the punch bowl just as the party gets going — and he's tightening into a boom over the President's loud objections. Money is getting dearer. Lock in long-term loans at today's rates while you can, or stay short and ride the tightening up.
candidates.historicalNote: William McChesney Martin chaired the Fed 1951-1970 and championed 'leaning against the wind' — tightening to head off inflation, including the courageous December 1965 discount-rate hike over President Johnson's objections.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: chunk 007 (pp.64-65, Martin and the 'punch bowl' / leaning-against-the-wind doctrine); corroborated by Meltzer chunk 007 pp.454-458 (Dec 1965 4-3 hike over LBJ)

candidates.title: America Buys on Time
candidates.description: Every suburban kitchen wants a humming refrigerator, a television, a second car — and most families intend to pay for them in monthly installments. Consumer credit is exploding and the margins are fat. Build out an installment-finance operation now and you ride the most profitable wave of the decade; overextend to the wrong borrowers and the repos pile up in your lot.
candidates.historicalNote: Consumer installment credit expanded rapidly across the 1950s as mass-produced durable goods and the suburban household drove demand for financing.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: general; Levy KB extraction did not reach postwar consumer-credit chapters

candidates.title: The 3-6-3 Banker
candidates.description: Borrow at three percent, lend at six, be on the golf course by three. In the most sheltered, fail-proof business in America, the safe play is to do nothing clever at all and let Regulation Q guarantee your spread. But a competitor across town is getting restless — and complacency is its own kind of risk when the world finally changes.
candidates.historicalNote: Mid-century commercial banking was among the safest businesses in America — only a handful of the nation's ~14,000 banks failed in a typical year — epitomized by the '3-6-3' stereotype of guaranteed spreads and short hours.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: chunk 007 (pp.59-62, banking as the safest, nearly fail-proof business; ~14,000 banks, few failures); '3-6-3' phrase is the common stereotype, not verbatim in source

candidates.title: The Correspondent Network
candidates.description: Hundreds of small country banks need a big-city partner to clear their checks, hold their reserves, and place their surplus funds. Become the correspondent of choice and you command a river of cheap deposits and loan participations from across the heartland. The relationships are sticky — win them now and rivals will struggle to pry them loose.
candidates.historicalNote: Under the fragmented unit-banking system, city banks built correspondent relationships with thousands of small banks, supplying clearing, reserves, and loan participations — a stable, low-risk source of funds and fees.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: general structure of mid-century US banking; Greider notes ~14,000 banks (chunk 007) as backdrop

candidates.title: The London Dollar Desk
candidates.description: A vast pool of expatriate dollars has collected in London, beyond the reach of Regulation Q and reserve requirements. Open a London branch and you can pay market rates for funds and lend them worldwide with a freedom Washington would never allow at home. The aggressive money-center men are building empires on this offshore river; the timid stay home and watch.
candidates.historicalNote: The Eurodollar market — an unregulated offshore pool of dollars built up over the postwar decades — let US banks fund and lend free of domestic ceilings; by the late 1970s US banks held over $400 billion in overseas offices.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: chunk 007 (p.58, Eurodollar as a '51st state' / >$400B in US banks' overseas offices). Wriston/Citibank attribution is from general knowledge — not named in this KB extraction

candidates.title: The Plastic Gamble
candidates.description: A California bank has mailed unsolicited charge cards to tens of thousands of customers at once, and the early losses are ugly — fraud, deadbeats, furious cardholders. But the survivors are minting money on revolving interest. Launch your own card program into this mess and you might own the consumer's wallet for a generation, or you might mail your way straight into a write-off.
candidates.historicalNote: BankAmericard launched in 1958 via mass unsolicited card 'drops' that produced heavy early fraud and delinquency losses before revolving-credit economics made the product hugely profitable.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: general credit-card history; not in KB extractions (Levy postwar chapters missing)

candidates.title: The Interbank Card Alliance
candidates.description: A group of banks shut out of the California card network have banded together to launch their own rival system. Joining means instant national acceptance for your cardholders — and a share of an arms race that will define consumer banking. Sit it out and your card is a local curiosity; jump in and you're betting on plastic against the establishment.
candidates.historicalNote: In 1966 a consortium of banks formed the Interbank Card Association (the future Master Charge / Mastercard) to compete with BankAmericard, accelerating the national card duopoly.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: general credit-card history; not in KB extractions

candidates.title: Financing the Suburbs' Schools
candidates.description: Every new suburb needs schools, sewers, and water towers, and the municipalities are issuing tax-exempt bonds to pay for them. Underwrite and hold this paper and you earn a tax-advantaged yield your wealthiest clients will love — until a credit tightening freezes the municipal market and you're stuck holding bonds nobody wants to bid on.
candidates.historicalNote: Postwar suburbanization drove a boom in municipal bond issuance for schools and infrastructure; banks held large tax-exempt municipal portfolios that became hard to sell during credit crunches.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: 1966 crunch passage notes decline in lending for housing and state/local governments (p.741); municipal-boom framing from general knowledge

candidates.title: The 1966 Crunch Starves Housing
candidates.description: The Fed has tightened and Regulation Q won't let you pay enough to keep deposits from fleeing to Treasury bills. The thrifts are starved, mortgage money has vanished, and housing starts are collapsing. Your home-lending pipeline is bone dry. Ration credit to your best builders and ride it out, or chase deposits you can't legally afford to keep.
candidates.historicalNote: In 1966 the interest-rate structure let commercial banks drain deposits from thrifts, causing a major decline in housing and state/local lending; housing starts fell about 21% that year.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: p.741 (line 1221, retrospective on 1966: banks drained deposits, housing starts fell 21%); Meltzer 1951 doc pp.454-460 on the Dec 1965 trigger

candidates.title: The Promissory-Note Dodge
candidates.description: Regulation Q caps what you can pay for deposits — so a Boston bank has started issuing short-term promissory notes that are deposits in everything but name, paying market rates the ceiling forbids. Copy the trick and you keep your corporate funds from fleeing; the regulators are already drafting a response. How far ahead of the rulebook are you willing to run?
candidates.historicalNote: To evade Regulation Q ceilings, banks innovated around the rules — e.g., First National Bank of Boston issued promissory notes (1964) that functioned as market-rate deposits — a pattern of regulatory arbitrage that recurred through the 1960s.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: footnote 320, p.454 (line 792): First National Bank of Boston promissory notes, Sept 1964

candidates.title: Guns, Butter, and Creeping Inflation
candidates.description: Washington is paying for a war in Asia and a Great Society at home without raising taxes, and prices are starting to climb in a way they haven't for a generation. The real value of every fixed-rate loan on your books is quietly eroding. Reprice toward shorter, floating-rate credits now — or watch inflation transfer your margin to your borrowers.
candidates.historicalNote: Financing Vietnam and Great Society programs without tax increases drove inflation from roughly 1.6% in 1965 toward 6% by 1970, eroding the real value of fixed-rate loan portfolios.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: chunk 008 (p.68, JFK tax cuts + LBJ Vietnam = fiscal expansion); Meltzer notes CPI ~6% when Martin left in 1970 (1951 doc p.522)

candidates.title: The One-Bank Holding Company Land Grab
candidates.description: Before Congress closes the loophole, the biggest banks are racing to wrap themselves in one-bank holding companies and buy their way into leasing, data processing, insurance — businesses a bank could never own directly. Move now and you diversify your earnings beyond the spread; move late and the new law freezes you out.
candidates.historicalNote: In the late 1960s big banks rushed to form one-bank holding companies to enter non-banking businesses before the 1970 amendments to the Bank Holding Company Act closed the loophole.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: p.831 (lines 2831, 2839) surge in holding-company applications; 1970 BHC Act amendments

candidates.title: The Penn Central Paper Run
candidates.description: The nation's seventh-largest corporation — a railroad colossus — cannot roll over its commercial paper and has just filed for reorganization. Now every lender in the forty-billion-dollar paper market is asking which borrower is next, and the run is spreading. Companies shut out of the paper market are storming your loan officers for emergency credit lines. Extend a lifeline to the right names and you make a fortune; back the wrong one and you join them.
candidates.historicalNote: Penn Central — the 7th-largest US corporation — failed to roll over its commercial paper and filed for reorganization on June 21, 1970, threatening contagion in the ~$40 billion commercial-paper market; the Fed opened the discount window and suspended Regulation Q ceilings on large CDs so banks could replace fleeing paper with loans.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: 'The Penn Central Failure,' pp.605-607 (lines 1698-1724): 7th-largest corp, filed June 21 1970, $40B CP market, Fed opened discount window + suspended Reg Q on large CDs; NY Fed estimated $7-10B reserves needed

candidates.title: Reg Q Lifted on the Big CDs
candidates.description: To stop the Penn Central panic from freezing corporate America, the Fed has suspended Regulation Q ceilings on large certificates of deposit. Overnight you can pay whatever it takes to pull in jumbo corporate deposits and re-lend them to firms shut out of the paper market. The funding window is wide open — but everyone is bidding, and the spread you capture depends on your nerve.
candidates.historicalNote: During the June 1970 Penn Central crisis the Fed suspended Regulation Q ceilings on large-denomination CDs, letting banks bid competitively for corporate funds to backstop borrowers losing access to commercial paper.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: pp.605-607 (Reg Q ceilings on large CDs suspended; Gov. Mitchell the chief proponent of removing CD ceilings)

candidates.title: The Gold Drain
candidates.description: Foreign central banks hold far more dollars than America has gold to back them, and they are quietly asking to convert. Every shipment of bullion out of Fort Knox tightens the noose on the thirty-five-dollar peg. A bank with foreign-exchange exposure must decide: trust the dollar holds, or quietly hedge against the day Washington slams the gold window shut.
candidates.historicalNote: US liquid liabilities to foreigners exceeded the gold stock from 1960 and reached roughly 4x by 1970 (gold $11.1B vs liabilities $43.3B), steadily undermining the $35/oz convertibility commitment.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: Ch.5, Table 5.1 p.687 (lines 209-221): 1960 gold 17.8 / liabilities 21.0 -> 1970 gold 11.1 / liabilities 43.3; two-tier gold market March 1968

candidates.title: Camp David: The Gold Window Slams
candidates.description: The President has gone to Camp David with fifteen advisers and emerged to announce the unthinkable: the dollar is no longer convertible to gold, plus a wage-price freeze and a ten-percent surcharge on imports. The fixed-rate world that has ordered international banking since the war is over. Your foreign-exchange book is suddenly live ammunition — currencies will float, and the steady ones will get violent.
candidates.historicalNote: On August 15, 1971, after a Camp David meeting, Nixon announced the New Economic Policy: closing the gold window, a 90-day wage-price freeze, and a 10% import surcharge — ending Bretton Woods fixed convertibility and ushering in floating exchange rates.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: Ch.6 pp.753-775 (lines 1387-1763): Camp David Aug 13 1971, NEP Aug 15 (gold window closed, 90-day freeze, 10% surcharge); Connally drove it, Burns opposed closing the window

candidates.title: Floating Currencies, Flooding Dollars
candidates.description: With the dollar cut loose from gold, the offshore Eurodollar pool is swelling as nervous foreign central banks dump dollars into the market. The bank with a London desk can intermediate the flood — borrowing cheap, lending across borders, profiting from the very turbulence that terrifies everyone else. The currency swings are brutal, and a wrong-way position can erase a year of fees overnight.
candidates.historicalNote: After the 1971 dollar float, foreign official dollar accumulation and currency turbulence swelled the Eurodollar market; banks with offshore operations intermediated the flows. In the first nine months of 1971 foreign claims rose about $21 billion.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: Ch.5 (foreign claims +$21B in first nine months of 1971; 1971 capital-account deficit ~$30B)

candidates.title: The Overreacher's Ascent
candidates.description: A mid-sized bank across the region is growing at a breakneck pace — buying Eurodollar funds aggressively, speculating in foreign exchange, paying top rates for hot money. Its chief is the toast of the trade press. You can chase the same strategy and match its dazzling growth, or remember that every banking generation produces one spectacular blow-up and resolve not to be it.
candidates.historicalNote: By the early 1970s some aggressive banks funded rapid growth with hot Eurodollar money and foreign-exchange speculation — a strategy that fed the 1974 Franklin National and Herstatt failures, the first major postwar bank collapses.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: general (foreshadows existing live evt_franklin_national 1974); Eurodollar context corroborated by Greider chunk 007

candidates.title: The White House Leans on the Fed
candidates.description: The President wants cheap money for an election year and is leaning hard on the central bank to deliver it. The Fed chairman is wavering. Rates may be about to fall whether the economy needs it or not — a gift to borrowers today, kindling for inflation tomorrow. Position for easy money and the boom, or brace for the reckoning that always follows a political rate cut.
candidates.historicalNote: Chairman Arthur Burns presided over an easy-money boom ahead of the 1972 election — money growth ran high in the months before the vote — widely seen as helping re-elect Nixon and contributing to the inflation that followed.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: chunk 008 (pp.67-68: Burns's 1972 pump-priming, money growth ~11% three months before the election, 'embarrassing odor')

candidates.title: The Pension Money Pours In
candidates.description: Corporate America is funding generous defined-benefit pensions, and somebody has to manage the billions piling up. Your trust department can win these mandates and earn steady fees on assets that only grow — the bluest of blue-chip business. Win the relationships now, before the wirehouses and insurers lock them up.
candidates.historicalNote: Postwar corporate defined-benefit pension funds grew rapidly; bank trust departments captured large management and custody mandates over fast-growing pension assets.

provenance.source_chunk_or_page: general; not in KB extractions (postwar Levy chapters missing)
